questions.question: Why aren't cost governors enough on their own?
options: They are too slow to compute
They bound what the agent can spend, not what it can do inside the budget — exfiltrating a secret or deleting a resource is often cheap in tokens
They only work in the EU
They require a human to approve every action
questions.explanation: The expensive action is frequently the cheap one in tokens. Kill switches, circuit breakers, and canary tokens sit next to the cost layer to bound behavior, not just spend.

questions.question: What are the three detectors this lesson covers and their granularity?
options: Firewall, proxy, VPN — all network-level
Kill switch (disables the whole agent), circuit breaker (pauses one offending path), canary token (bait whose access is the alarm)
Logging, metrics, tracing — all observability
Linter, type-checker, test runner
questions.explanation: All three are pre-LLM engineering — feature-flag kills, Nygard-style breakers, classical honeytokens. What's new is the attack surface: agents read untrusted content, edit memory, and compose safe actions into unsafe ones, so the detectors must not trust the agent's self-report.

questions.question: What property must a correct kill switch have?
options: The agent can toggle it for self-correction
The agent can read it but cannot set it off — it lives in a system the agent's credentials cannot write, is checked on every consequential action, and re-enabling is an explicit human act
It auto-resets after a timeout
It only checks at startup
questions.explanation: A kill switch the agent can flip is not a kill switch. It must be outside the edit surface (managed flag, signed config, OS-level kill), checked per consequential action, and require deliberate human re-enable — never an automatic timeout.

questions.question: How does a canary token work, and what makes a bad one?
options: It encrypts secrets; a bad one uses weak encryption
It is bait the agent has no legitimate reason to touch (fake credential, sentinel DB record), so access is the alarm — a canary the agent has a real reason to touch is just noise
It caps the token budget; a bad one is set too high
It is a backup of the agent's memory
questions.explanation: A fake AWS key with no privilege makes exfiltration instantly attributable; a fake memory entry appearing in later output proves memory exfiltration. Canary design is workflow-specific precisely because a legitimately-touched canary generates false alarms.

questions.question: Why layer statistical detectors (EWMA, CUSUM) with hard constitutional limits?
options: Statistical detectors are always more accurate
Adaptive detectors track a moving baseline — a feature for legitimate drift, a bug for adversarial drift a patient attacker shifts slowly; hard limits like "never email secrets@" don't bend
Hard limits are faster to compute
Constitutional limits replace the need for detectors
questions.explanation: A slow attacker can move the statistical baseline under the alarm. The layering: statistical detectors catch most noise, and non-negotiable hard limits catch the attack that deliberately walked past them.
